result:
There is a very specific kind of magic in choosing to get lost. We spend so much of our modern lives tethered to destinations, marching frantically from point A to point B with our eyes fixed on the glowing maps in our hands. We optimize our routes. We calculate our arrival times. We rush. But when you turn off the GPS and take a left turn simply because the streetlights are casting a golden hue over the cobblestones, you rediscover the quiet joy of the undefined.

Yesterday, I wandered into a part of the city I had never seen. It wasn’t a trendy tourist destination; it was just a neighborhood existing quietly in the afternoon sun. I found a dusty bookshop where the spines were sun-bleached, and the owner played jazz on a record player that skipped every few seconds, adding a rhythmic stutter to the melody. I sat on a green iron bench and watched a man paint his front door the color of an unripe plum.

None of this was on an itinerary. None of it will change the world. But for twenty perfect minutes, I was a witness to a reality happening completely independent of my own. It is a profound relief to realize the world keeps spinning in its intricate, beautiful way when you aren't watching. Getting lost isn't about losing yourself; it’s about finding the hidden pockets of the universe that do not require your attention to exist, and that is where true freedom resides.
